title-group.article-title: Application of ultrasound and ultrasound-responsive materials in wounds: A systematic review

abstract.p: Wound healing is a multifaceted biological process that is highly susceptible to disruption by numerous internal and external factors, resulting in delayed tissue repair. Recent advancements have highlighted the therapeutic potential of ultrasound, especially when integrated with emerging smart materials, which together offer promising strategies for enhancing wound care outcomes. Low- and high-intensity ultrasound (US) use mechanisms such as cavitation, acoustic streaming, and radiation forces to disrupt biofilms, improve drug permeability, and promote tissue regeneration. When combined with US-responsive materials, such as scaffolds, piezoelectric composites, microbubbles, microneedles, and flexible patches, these systems enable targeted functions, including controlled drug release, antimicrobial activity, and enhanced deep tissue penetration. These responsive materials effectively overcome the limitations of conventional wound dressings, which often suffer from poor drug bioavailability, susceptibility to infection, and a lack of personalized therapeutic capabilities. This review systematically explores 5 categories of advanced materials, with a focus on their synergistic interactions with ultrasound to enhance wound healing outcomes. By elucidating the interactions between material-US and their translational implications, this review offers new perspectives to advance the clinical development of precision-based, next-generation wound therapies.

abstract.title: Graphical abstract
abstract.p: Ultrasound is utilized in wound healing through direct and indirect multidimensional approaches. Through acoustic mechanisms, ultrasound locally modifies the wound microenvironment, establishing favorable conditions for optimal healing. Indirect effects are mediated by ultrasound - responsive materials (scaffolds, microbubbles, microneedles, piezoelectric materials, ultrasonic patches), which convert ultrasonic energy to enable the coupling of acoustics with advanced materials.Unlabelled Image

list-item.p: Low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (LIPUS) promotes wound healing by stimulating cellular repair and angiogenesis.
High-intensity focused ultrasound (HIFU) facilitates wound healing by enabling precise debridement and biofilm disruption.
Ultrasound-responsive material platforms demonstrate that their integrated functions cater to diverse wound healing demands.
Multifaceted applications of ultrasound in wound healing as well as existing challenges in clinical translation.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The clinical management of acute and chronic wounds, including common types like burns, surgical injuries, diabetes, and pressure injuries, presents significant challenges due to their complex pathological profiles. These wounds typically feature persistent inflammation, impaired tissue regeneration, and microbial dysbiosis (Freedman et al., 2023; Martin et al., 2024). Chronic non-healing wounds impose a substantial yet often overlooked burden on global healthcare systems, significantly affecting both economic resources and patient quality of life. The increasing prevalence of systemic conditions, such as diabetes mellitus, coupled with the aging global population, has elevated chronic wounds into a pressing clinical, societal, and economic concern. Consequently, there is a critical need for wound dressing materials that can dynamically adapt to the heterogeneous wound environment, offer high biocompatibility, and support personalized therapeutic interventions (Perez et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2018).

The wound healing process progresses through four well-defined phases: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and tissue remodeling. However, chronic wounds often develop due to a combination of factors, including persistent inflammation, cellular dysfunction, and microbial infections. For instance, in patients with diabetes, macrophage polarization is impaired, while neutrophils release excessive neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs), both of which hinder tissue repair (Peña and Martin, 2024). The hyperglycemic microenvironment further compromises healing by inhibiting fibroblast proliferation and promoting senescent cell accumulation (Mieczkowski et al., 2022). Additionally, antibiotic-resistant bacterial biofilms perpetuate inflammation by persistently activating immune signaling pathways, such as Toll-like receptors (TLRs), thereby creating a hostile and dysregulated wound environment (Moura et al., 2013; N.D., 2024; Sen et al., 2021; Uberoi et al., 2024).

To address the complexities of wound repair in diverse physiological environments, biomaterials must be capable of dynamically adapting to external stimuli. Stimuli-responsive materials have consequently emerged as promising solutions, capable of maintaining tissue homeostasis and promoting repair through integrated sensing, communication, and energy transduction mechanisms. Among various actuation methods, ultrasound (US) has emerged as a particularly effective stimulus, offering precise spatiotemporal control and the advantage of non-invasive application. Additionally, an ideal wound dressing should possess a combination of tailored physical and biochemical properties. Key physical characteristics include suitable mechanical strength, elasticity, adaptability to wound contours, breathability, and fluid adsorption capacity (Fig.1). Equally important are its biochemical properties, such as resistance to foreign body response (FBR) and controlled biodegradability. Functionally, the dressing should actively support healing by promoting fibroblast growth, enhancing epithelial regeneration, facilitating vascularization, encouraging collagen deposition, modulating immune responses, and preventing infection (Fig.1).Fig. 1
The principle of ultrasonic response, ultrasonic-responsive materials and material properties.Fig. 1

As a non-invasive modality, ultrasound promotes wound healing through diverse bioacoustic mechanisms. Its capacity to precisely regulate biological responses via acoustic energy parameter modulation establishes a robust foundation for ultrasound-responsive biomaterials, leveraging key physical phenomena – including cavitation, acoustic radiation forces (ARF), microstreaming, and scattering – to achieve precise control over composite material components (Sun et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2023). The design of ultrasound-responsive materials enable diverse functionalities, including mechanical actuation, biosensing, targeted payload delivery, and initiation of chemical or biological reactions (Balcerak-Woźniak et al., 2024; Huang et al., 2023; Huang et al., 2017b; Zhao et al., 2013). This review examines ultrasound's capacity to promote wound healing through precisely regulated bioacoustic mechanisms, synergized with ultrasound-responsive biomaterials to achieve multifaceted therapeutic enhancement (Fig.1). We systematically categorize biomaterials into five functional classes: scaffolds, piezoelectric materials, microbubbles, microneedles, and ultrasound patches (Fig.1). These material platforms leverage distinct actuation principles and acoustic parameter modulation to remodel pathological wound microenvironments. Finally, this review contributes several key innovations to the field. First, it establishes a unified therapeutic framework by concurrently evaluating and comparing five distinct classes of US-responsive materials—scaffolds, piezoelectrics, microbubbles, microneedles, and flexible patches—for wound healing, enabling a systematic elucidation of their synergistic mechanisms (Christ et al., 2025; Jeyavelkumaran et al., 2025). Second, it bridges fundamental material design principles (e.g., stiffness, porosity, drug-loading) and ultrasound parameters (LIPUS vs. HIFU) to address the distinct clinical challenges of acute, chronic, and infected wounds, such as biofilm eradication, angiogenesis, and immunomodulation (Dai et al., 2022; Yao et al., 2025). This enables a precise matching of the “material-energy-application” triad. Finally, it significantly extends the discussion on translational barriers, moving beyond conventional topics of biocompatibility and manufacturability to incorporate critical analyses of regulatory pathways, device portability, cost-effectiveness, and clinical trial design (Aboy et al., 2024). This expanded perspective aims to provide a practical roadmap for bridging the gap between laboratory innovation and clinical adoption.

The literature search for this systematic review was conducted using the electronic databases PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, and IEEE Xplore. The search terms included combinations of: (“ultrasound-responsive” OR “sonodynamic”) AND (“wound healing” OR “chronic wound” OR “diabetic ulcer”) AND (“scaffold” OR “microneedle” OR “microbubble” OR “piezoelectric” OR “patch”). The search was limited to peer-reviewed articles published in English between 2015 and 2024, with seminal earlier works included for foundational concepts. Titles and abstracts were screened independently by two authors based on relevance to US mechanisms, material design, and wound healing outcomes. Full texts of selected articles were assessed for inclusion, with any discrepancies resolved by a third author. The reference lists of key review articles were also hand-searched to identify additional relevant studies.

sec.title: The normal process of wound healing and refractory factors
The process of wound healing
sec.p: Wound healing is a multifaceted biological process governed by the coordinated interaction of various cell types and signaling mediators over time. The process proceeds through four overlapping but distinct phases: hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and tissue remodeling (Martin et al., 2024).

Following skin injury, hemostasis is initiated immediately as activated platelets aggregate to form a fibrin clot (coagulation), sealing the wound and releasing signaling molecules (Peña and Martin, 2024). This process rapidly triggers the inflammatory phase cascade, typically within hours. Neutrophils are the first responders recruited through extravasation from nearby blood vessels, followed by directed migration into the wound site to clear cellular debris and pathogens(Ferguson and O'Kane, 2004). Macrophages subsequently infiltrate the wound and become the dominant immune cells, modulating the inflammatory environment and orchestrating the transition to tissue repair. Over the following days, these macrophages facilitate the formation of granulation tissue to replace damaged dermal and hypodermal structures (Sharifiaghdam et al., 2022). This intricate phase of wound healing is marked by angiogenesis, which is driven by pro-angiogenic factors such as vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), which promotes the formation of new blood vessels (Sousa et al., 2022). Fibroblasts are recruited to the wound site, where they migrate, proliferate, and deposit extracellular matrix (ECM) proteins, primarily collagen, through mechanisms involving cytoskeletal remodeling and cell adhesion molecules (Boraldi et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2018). Simultaneously, re-epithelialization is initiated as keratinocytes from the wound edges migrate across the developing granulation tissue. These cells proliferate within the epidermal proliferative zone and contribute to the reformation of the epidermal layer and basement membrane, ultimately restoring the skin's surface barrier. The active cellular responses gradually subside as the wound edges converge and closure is achieved. The final remodeling and resolution stage extends over weeks to several months. During this prolonged phase, superfluous cells are eliminated through apoptosis, while the initially dense and disorganized collagen matrix undergoes enzymatic degradation, reorganization, and cross-linking, leading to the formation of a more structured extracellular matrix (Huang et al., 2022; Peña and Martin, 2024; Shi et al., 2023; Veith et al., 2019). Concurrently, neovascular regression occurs as excess blood vessels formed during earlier stages are resorbed. This extensive tissue remodeling ultimately results in scar formation, which differs structurally and functionally from native skin (Clark, 2021; Mascharak et al., 2021). The wound healing timeline typically follows a sequence: clot formation within hours, inflammation and early reparative phases over several days, granulation tissue formation and re-epithelialization within weeks, and prolonged remodeling extending into months.

sec.title: The complexity of wound healing
sec.p: Wound healing is a highly complex biological process that poses substantial clinical challenges. Wounds are generally classified as either acute or chronic based on their etiology and underlying pathophysiology. Acute wounds, such as surgical incisions, thermal wounds, abrasions, bite wounds, penetrating injuries, and displacements, typically heal within 2 weeks. In contrast, chronic wounds, such as vascular ulcers, pressure injuries, and diabetic foot ulcers, often resist healing due to persistent pathological factors (Uberoi et al., 2024). The wound healing process is influenced by multiple factors, including wound type and severity, the patient's overall health status, age, nutritional status, and comorbidities, such as diabetes or vascular diseases. These factors often disrupt normal healing processes (von Stebut and Helbig, 2023). Impairment at any stage of the healing process can significantly delay recovery, often extending beyond 3 months or resulting in complete failure to heal in severe cases.

Multiple pathological mechanisms, including inflammatory arrest, fibroblast senescence, oxidative stress, infection, and biofilm formation, often cause abnormal wound healing. Persistent tissue damage occurs due to impaired growth factors, extracellular matrix degradation, and stromal cell dysfunction, often exacerbated by the dominance of M1-type macrophages that release pro-inflammatory cytokines. A critical factor in chronic wounds is dysfunctional oxygen species clearance. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) induce direct cellular damage, causing dysfunction and apoptosis, while also perpetuating inflammation through inflammatory pathway activation, further disrupting healing [28–30]. Additionally, vascular injury restricts blood flow, reducing nutrient and oxygen delivery essential for tissue regeneration. Concurrent nerve damage compromises sensory feedback, thereby delaying wound deterioration detection and subsequent treatment (Everett and Mathioudakis, 2018; Kang et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2019).

sec.title: Therapeutic ultrasound and US-responsive materials for wound healing
Definition and therapeutic spectrum of ultrasound for wound healing: LIPUS vs. HIFU
sec.p: Therapeutic ultrasound for wound management is broadly categorized by its energy density and primary bioeffects into two distinct modalities: Low-Intensity Pulsed Ultrasound (LIPUS) and High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU) (Liang et al., 2024; Qin et al., 2025). This distinction is fundamental, as it dictates the mechanism of action, safety profile, and clinical application.

Low-intensity pulsed ultrasound (LIPUS) is a specific form of low-intensity ultrasound (LIUS) that delivers pulsed acoustic waves to minimize thermal effects—typically maintaining intensities below 3 W/cm2—thereby preventing potential tissue damage. Its therapeutic effects are primarily mechanobiological, stemming from acoustic radiation force, microstreaming, and stable cavitation (Fan et al., 2024; Li et al., 2023). These forces exert mild mechanical stimuli on cells and tissues, activating intracellular signaling pathways that promote cell proliferation, migration, angiogenesis, and collagen synthesis while modulating inflammation (Bergmeister et al., 2018; de Oliveira Perrucini et al., 2020; Ichijo et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2024; Peng et al., 2020; Percival et al., 2024; Ren et al., 2018). LIPUS is thus suited for accelerating the repair process in chronic wounds (e.g., venous and diabetic ulcers) and facilitating soft tissue regeneration without causing thermal damage.

High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU) delivers high energy densities (often >10 W/cm2) to a precise focal volume. The primary mechanisms are thermal ablation (coagulative necrosis) and mechanical disruption via inertial cavitation and shock waves (Bachu et al., 2021; Cambronero et al., 2023; Hsiao et al., 2016). In wound care, HIFU's ability to non-invasively fragment necrotic tissue, disrupt deep bacterial biofilms, and achieve hemostasis is leveraged for wound debridement and preparation of the wound bed for healing (Granick et al., 2020; Kataoka et al., 2021). It is primarily an ablative or disruptive tool used for specific indications within a broader treatment plan. Table 1 provides a concise technical comparison of LIPUS and HIFU, outlining their defining parameters, biological effects, and clinical wound applications to guide the reader through the subsequent discussion of material interactions.Table 1
Technical comparison of LIPUS and HIFU in wound management.Table 1
ParameterLow-Intensity Pulsed Ultrasound (LIPUS)High-Intensity Focused Ultrasound (HIFU)
Typical Intensity< 3 W/cm2> 10 W/cm2
Primary MechanismNon-thermal (Mechanical: Acoustic streaming, radiation force, stable cavitation)Thermal ablation & Mechanical disruption (Inertial cavitation, shock waves)
Key Biological EffectsStimulates cellular activity (fibroblasts, endothelial cells), promotes angiogenesis, enhances collagen deposition, modulates inflammation.Coagulative necrosis, emulsification of necrotic tissue, physical disruption of biofilm structures, hemostasis.
Primary Wound IndicationsChronic wound healing (venous, diabetic ulcers), stimulation of surgical incision repair, soft tissue regeneration.Debridement of non-viable tissue, ablation of hypergranulation tissue, disruption of deep-seated biofilms in chronic osteomyelitis or abscesses.
Device PortabilityHigh. Numerous handheld, battery-operated devices are commercially available for clinic or home use.Variable/Low. Often console-based systems. Portable, dedicated wound-debridement HIFU units are emerging but not yet widespread.
Treatment Cost ConsiderationModerate (device cost + disposable applicators). Reusable handheld devices can reduce long-term cost.High (significant capital equipment cost). Procedure cost includes specialized transducers and coupling systems.

sec.title: Two types of ultrasound for wound healing
sec.p: US therapy has emerged as a promising non-invasive modality for wound healing. The therapeutic applications of US are primarily categorized into LIPUS and HIFU. The underlying mechanisms and biological outcomes of these two approaches differ significantly. LIPUS delivers US at low intensities using short duty cycles, alternating between ON and OFF phases to elicit bioeffect without causing thermal damage. Studies have shown that LIPUS is effective in accelerating the healing of surgical incisions, tendon and nerve injuries, and bone fractures [50–56]. In contrast, HIFU employs focused high-intensity US to generate high temperatures in the lesion area by concentrating sound waves, thereby inducing coagulative necrosis of target tissues. This thermal ablation technique is primarily used for tumor treatment and is designed to minimize collateral damage to the surrounding healthy tissues.

LIUS exerts mechanical effects that modulate cellular behavior by activating various intracellular signaling pathways. These include integrin-mediated mTOR, Rho/ROCK/Src/ERK, NF-κB, and PI 3-kinase pathways, which are involved in regulating proliferation, differentiation, immune responses, and gene expression across different cell types (Kusuyama et al., 2014; Nakao et al., 2014; Xia et al., 2017; Xin et al., 2016). LIUS has demonstrated therapeutic potential in several contexts, including the acceleration of fracture healing, promotion of soft tissue regeneration, suppression of inflammatory responses, and emerging applications in neuromodulation (Bajpai et al., 2018; Boerman et al., 2023; Maan et al., 2014). Vander Horst et al. (2021) investigated the effects of pulsed US at a frequency of 1 MHz and a pressure amplitude of 0.4 MPa on wound healing in diabetic mice. Their findings showed that this US protocol significantly enhanced granulation tissue formation, accelerated wound contraction, and promoted epithelialization. Clinical evidence now substantiates the therapeutic efficacy of LIPUS in venous ulcer management. In a pivotal randomized controlled trial by Boerman et al. (2023), 48 patients with chronic venous ulcers were randomly assigned to either an active LIPUS treatment group (20 kHz, 100 mW/cm2) or a sham control group that received inactive US exposure. Treatment was administered over a one-week cycle. Gene set enrichment analysis of the final tissue samples revealed that LIPUS significantly upregulated gene sets associated with intercellular signaling pathways and concurrently downregulated gene sets associated with inflammatory responses.

High-intensity ultrasound (HIUS) operates at significantly greater energy levels than LIUS, typically reaching hundreds of watts per square centimeter (W/cm2). Elevated energy input induces a more pronounced cavitation effect. Clinically, HIFU is primarily employed for the thermal ablation treatment of solid tumors (Bachu et al., 2021; Cambronero et al., 2023; Hsiao et al., 2016). Beyond tumor ablation, the intense shear force and shock waves produced by HIFU can fragment and emulsify necrotic tissue and disrupt bacterial biofilm within wounds. This mechanical action also stimulates cellular activity, enabling deeper wound cleaning and establishing a favorable environment for subsequent healing processes (Granick et al., 2020; Kataoka et al., 2021). In addition, several studies have indicated that HIFU may enhance collagen synthesis and structural remodeling during the healing process. These effects are potentially mediated by increased levels of transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-β) and reduced matrix metalloproteinase-3 (MMP-3) activity. These molecular effects position HIFU as a promising modality for promoting the structural integrity of healing tissue during the remodeling phase (Lee et al., 2017).

Despite these promising clinical outcomes, critical knowledge gaps remain regarding the optimization of therapeutic US parameters for wound healing. Key uncertainties persist regarding exposure conditions, such as US frequency, amplitude, intensity, spatial distribution, and treatment duration, as well as the underlying biological mechanisms of action (Alkahtani et al., 2017; Bekara et al., 2018). A more comprehensive understanding of how therapeutic US facilitates wound closure is essential to optimize device settings and treatment protocols. Such knowledge could enhance clinical outcomes by improving healing efficiency and consistency across patient populations. To achieve this, further well-designed clinical trials are urgently needed.

sec.title: Mechanism of ultrasound-based wound healing
sec.p: Through the core acoustic mechanisms of cavitation, acoustic streaming, acoustic radiation force, and thermal effects, ultrasound facilitates non-invasive wound healing by converting sound energy into mechanical, thermal, and electrical energy. This energy conversion simultaneously enables structural modulation of ultrasound-responsive materials, establishing an integrated therapeutic framework that couples biological regulation with targeted energy delivery and material response.

sec.title: Cavitation
sec.p:
Ultrasonic cavitation is a core mechanism driving the functionality of advanced drug delivery systems, which utilizes the dynamic behavior of US-activated microbubbles to disrupt biofilms and enhance the penetration and efficacy of therapeutic agents. US-mediated transdermal delivery offers precise spatiotemporal control, effectively functioning as a non-invasive “remote controller” and on-demand “trigger” (Epstein-Barash et al., 2010). Acoustic oscillations induce bubble nucleation and collapse, converting US energy into kinetic motion. This process generates potent mechanical shear forces and localized thermal effects, which are categorized into two cavitation types: inertial cavitation (transient) and non-inertial cavitation (stable) (Fig. 2I). Microbubbles oscillate symmetrically at low US intensities in stable cavitation. In contrast, high-intensity US induces asymmetrical oscillations or complete microbubble collapse, generating strong shock waves or liquid microjets that can perforate nearby cell membranes, a phenomenon known as the sonoporation or sonopore effect (Abou-Elkacem et al., 2015; Tu and Alfred, 2022). This mechanical force can disrupt the cytoskeleton and compromise membrane integrity, including damage to vascular endothelial layers (Fig. 2I). The efficiency of US-induced membrane perforation depends on several variables, including acoustic parameters (e.g., frequency, pressure, pulse duration), the physicochemical properties of microbubbles, their proximity to cells, and the biomechanical characteristics of the target cells (Hulme, 2025).Fig. 2
Mechanisms of Ultrasonic-Induced Energy Conversion I. Cavitation mechanisms Reprinted with permission from Du et al. (2025). II. Energy conversion mechanisms of ultrasound: A) Bubble vibration-induced flow. B) Microstructure oscillation-driven flow. C) Acoustic streaming generated via wave propagation and attenuation. D) Acoustic radiation force Reprinted with permission from Athanassiadis et al. (2021). III. Acoustic radiation force assessment of attenuation-independent residual-to-saturation ratio Reprinted with permission from Abou-Elkacem et al. (2015). IV. Schematic representation of crystal structure responses to external forces. Reprinted with permission from Wei et al. (2025).Fig. 2

sec.title: Acoustic Streaming
sec.p: Acoustic streaming refers to the steady fluid flow generated by the propagation of acoustic waves resulting from the transfer of momentum from the wave to the fluid. This effect typically originates within the fluid boundary layer adjacent to vibrating bubbles or structures, where sharp velocity gradients lead to the dissipation of acoustic energy. The resulting shear-driven flow in this thin region is known as Schlichting streaming. Subsequently, this localized motion induces a broader, bulk fluid flow referred to as Rayleigh streaming (Fig. 2II A, 3II B). In viscous fluids, ultrasonic wave propagation is attenuated due to acoustic damping. The resulting momentum transfer generates a pressure gradient that drives the formation of stable vortices and establishes a steady-state streaming pattern (Fig. 2II C) (Athanassiadis et al., 2021).

sec.title: Acoustic Radiation Force (ARF)
sec.p: In contrast to acoustic streaming, which induces bulk fluid motion, the acoustic radiation force (ARF) acts directly on suspended particles. As a nonlinear phenomenon, ARF generation depends on US intensity, frequency, and the acoustic/geometric properties of the materials involved. This fundamental force enables precise spatial control of particles, cells, and microbubbles within acoustic fields, facilitating controlled drug delivery and bioeffect initiation. ARF enhances particle collision frequency between drug-loaded particles (Fig. 2II D), thereby increasing local drug concentration and enhancing therapeutic outcomes (Fateh et al., 2021; Mao et al., 2017). In one application, molecularly targeted microbubbles (green) were introduced into the bloodstream and exposed to a focused ARF pulse (Fig. 2III). This pulse propels the targeted microbubbles toward the vascular endothelium, thereby improving their specific binding. Following the cessation of ARF, flow-mediated shear forces detach loosely associated microbubbles, leaving only those that are firmly adhered to the endothelium (Fig. 2III) (Willmann et al., 2008).

sec.title: Piezoelectric effects
sec.p: The piezoelectric effect refers to the generation of electrical charge in response to mechanical stress (direct piezoelectric effect) or the induction of mechanical deformation under applied electric fields (converse piezoelectric effect). US-responsive piezoelectric materials are categorized into three groups: Crystalline materials (e.g., quartz), Ceramics (e.g., lead zirconate titanate, PZT), and Polymers (e.g., polyvinylidene fluoride, PVDF) (Alvarez-Lorenzo et al., 2023; Chorsi et al., 2019). The electromechanical properties of these materials are primarily evaluated using two parameters: the piezoelectric strain constant (d33) and voltage coefficient (g33). The d33 quantifies the material's ability to produce longitudinal strain per unit of applied electric field, reflecting its performance under the converse piezoelectric effect. In contrast, the g33 quantifies the electric potential generated per unit of mechanical stress, indicating the performance under the direct piezoelectric effect (Athanassiadis et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2022). These coefficients collectively determine the efficiency and responsiveness of piezoelectric materials in facilitating bidirectional energy conversion between the electrical and mechanical domains.

US exerts significant pressure on piezoelectric materials, combining the acoustic pressure transmitted through ultrasonic waves and the intense mechanical force generated during acoustic cavitation collapse (Huang et al., 2017a; Shi et al., 2011). In addition, ultrasonic waves induce periodic compressive stress that prevents the complete neutralization of piezoelectric polarization charges by free carriers. In the absence of external mechanical stress, the centers of positive and negative charges overlap in both centrosymmetric (square) and asymmetric (hexagonal) crystal structures, resulting in overall electrical neutrality. However, the centrosymmetric structures (left panel) lack piezoelectric properties due to their inherent symmetry. In contrast, applying compressive force to non-centrosymmetric structures induces the separation of charge centers, resulting in the formation of electric dipoles (Fig. 2IV). The collective alignment of these dipoles across multiple structural units generates a measurable macroscopic piezoelectric field (Wang et al., 2010). Furthermore, the stress induced by US can periodically modulate this internal electric field via piezoelectric transducers operating at various frequencies (Li et al., 2015).

sec.title: Ultrasound-responsive materials for wound healing
sec.p:
Emerging US-responsive materials can synergistically combine the advantages of controlled drug release and enhanced drug delivery efficiency under an applied US field. Recent advances in novel therapeutics, sonodynamic therapy, bioelectrical stimulation, and bioscaffolds have been significantly driven by the development of these advanced materials, thereby expanding the available wound management strategies. This article reviews the application of five US-responsive materials in wound healing (Table 2). Integrating ultrasound-responsive capabilities into established material platforms leverages US portability, controllability, and penetration depth to enable active drug delivery, enhancing wound healing efficacy with strong clinical translation potential (Guo et al., 2025; Shakya et al., 2024).Table 2

The wound healing effect was the best within 14 days, and it had a better therapeutic effect compared with the control group (healthy mice).(Ouyang et al., 2023)
MicroneedlesThe microneedle (MN) patch consists of sonosensitizer TiO₂-X, ·OH – responsive CO prodrug MPA - CO and glucose oxidase (GOX).1.75 W, 10 minLIUSInfected woundTiO₂ -X nanoparticles in microneedles act as acoustic sensitizers and generate ROS (•OH) under ultrasonic stimulation. These •OH react with the CO prodrug (MPA-CO), thereby releasing CO gas and achieving ultrasound-responsive CO release.In vivo experiment: The wound enclosure ratios were summarized to be 71.9%, which was notably higher than those observed with blank MN and MN@GOX@TiO2-X under US (40.7% and 39.7%, respectively) (diabetic mice).(Cao et al., 2025)
MicroneedlesSodium hyaluronate was used as the substrate and loaded ZnTCPP@ZnO Microneedle patches of nanoparticles1 MHz, 1.5 W/cm2, 15 minLIUSInfected woundUnder the action of ultrasonic waves, the acoustic cavitation effect and the interface effect of ZnTCPP@ZnO promote the separation of electrons and holes, activate oxygen to produce a large amount of ROS, thereby achieving antibacterial and repair promoting functions.In vivo experiment: After 7 days, the skin tissue of ZnTCPP@ZnO MNs + ultrasound group became thinner and had the fewest inflammatory cells [female Balb/c mice (8–10 weeks old)].(Xiang et al., 2023)
MicroneedlesEncapsulate CuO₂/TiO₂ heterostructured hyaluronic acid1 MHz, 1.0 W/cm2, 5 minLIUSInfected woundTriggered by ultrasound, it interacts with the components in material MN@GOX@TiO₂-X@CO through a cascade reaction. Eventually, the hydroxyl radical reacts with the ROS-responsive carbon monoxide prodrug (MPA-CO) to release CO gas, exerting antibacterial and angiogenesis promoting effects.In vivo experiment: MN@GOX@TiO₂-X@CO combined with ultrasound treatment significantly accelerated healing within 12 days (6-week-old BALB/c female mice).(Liang et al., 2023)
Flexible Ultrasonic PatchThe b-WPUE is composed of a γ-glycine/PVA piezoelectric film, Mo electrodes, Si PIN diodes, and PLA films.1 MHz, 0.1 W/cm2LIUSFull-thickness woundWhen excited by ultrasonic waves (1 MHz), the piezoelectric γ-glycine/PVA film vibrates and generates electricity. The device produces a stable output voltage of 220 mV/mm after rectification, which is sufficient for wound electrotherapy.In vivo experiment: The wounds in the b-WPUE + US group healed approximately 40% faster than those in the control group (female C57 mice).(Xue et al., 2024)
Flexible Ultrasonic PatchThe UEP is composed of an ultrasonic array, an energy exchange array, and an electric stimulation coil.420 kHz/Full-thickness woundThe ultrasonic energy is converted into pulse voltage (output range 2.4–19.9 V, instantaneous current 24–198 μA) through the energy exchange array (the positive piezoelectric effect of PZT-4).In vivo experiment: Although the surface healing rates of each group were similar, ultrasound-electrical coupling therapy (especially the HUE group) (Day 14, 97.22 ± 0.46%) significantly improved the healing quality by regulating the inflammatory microenvironment, optimizing collagen arrangement, and promoting hair follicle regeneration (8-week-old C57BL/6 male mice).(Chen et al., 2025b)
Flexible Ultrasonic PatchFlexible PDMS is used as the substrate, PZT-4 is used as the piezoelectric ceramic, and a thin layer of gel is used as the packaging and coupling layer.2.2 MHz,20 mW/cm2LIUSchronic refractory woundThe therapeutic ultrasound was focused on the target wound location through the inverse piezoelectric effect and patch shape under the condition of electrical stimulation.In vivo experiment: The wound healing rate in the flexible ultrasound patch treatment group was approximately 75%, and the healing time was reduced by 40% (type II diabetic rats).(Lyu et al., 2021)

sec.title: Ultrasound-responsive scaffolds
sec.p: The use of US-responsive scaffold materials in wound healing has demonstrated significant innovation and promising clinical potential (Wu et al., 2019; Xiao et al., 2024). In recent years, the smart integration of sonodynamic regulation mechanisms with functional material design has enabled these scaffolds to achieve spatiotemporal control over the release and dynamic degradation of therapeutic agents or bioactive factors.

The ultrasound-responsive heparin-binding domain (HBD) peptide hydrogel developed by Zong et al. (2024) uses the carboxylate (-COO-) in sodium alginate to form a crosslinked structure by liganding with calcium ions (Ca2+) to build a three-dimensional network skeleton of the hydrogel (Fig. 3I A). This cross-linking not only enhances the hydrogel's structural integrity but also allows controlled disruption under ultrasonic mechanical vibration and cavitation. Consequently, the hydrogel enables the on-demand release of silver ions (Ag+) and HBD peptides, which exhibit dual functionality: antimicrobial activity and promotion of wound regeneration (Fig. 3I B).Fig. 3
Triggering Mechanisms and Degradability of Ultrasound-Responsive Scaffolds. I. Synthesis and ultrasound-induced degradation of XA@Ag/H hydrogel: A) Schematic of the synthesis process and ultrasound-induced cargo release and hydrogel degradation. B) TEM images showing structural changes in hydrogels before and after ultrasound exposure. C) Release behavior of ultrasound-responsive hydrogel. Reprinted with permission from Wei et al. (2025). II. Schematic illustration of real-time monitoring and on-demand treatment of skin wounds using the ACPP hydrogel. Reprinted with permission from Huang et al. (2024). III. Effects of Ultrasound on scaffold morphology under non-vacuum conditions: A, B) SEM images of non-vacuumed samples at 0 and 15 min of exposure, respectively. SEM images indicated that the surfaces were significantly changed with sonication. C, D) Comparison of pore wall thickness in non-vacuumed samples, with histograms indicating that 15 min of sonication resulted in thinner pore walls than the control. Reprinted with permission from DeBari et al. (2021). IV Ultrasound-triggered nanoparticle release from collagen-GAG (CG) scaffolds containing alginate microparticles: A) In the first-generation scaffold, alginate microparticles embedded in CG scaffolds release nanoparticles upon ultrasound stimulation. B) In the second-generation scaffold, alginate is in discrete pockets within the CG matrix, allowing individual loading of different nanoparticles for release at distinct timepoints, and a schematic of the ultrasound-triggered release profile. Reprinted with permission from Ahmad et al. (2021).Fig. 3

Composite hydrogels enable real-time visual monitoring of wound infection status while facilitating precise, on-demand antibiotic delivery. Huang et al. (2024) developed a smart hydrogel dressing incorporating natural anthocyanins, which displays color changes in response to the pH of the wound environment (Fig. 3II). Acute healing wounds typically maintain a weakly acidic pH (5–6), whereas chronic wounds exhibit elevated pH (7–9) due to persistent inflammation. Upon infection, the pH rapidly exceeds 8.0, triggering a visible color transition in the hydrogel (purple → blue-green This shift can be quantitatively analyzed via RGB signal using a smartphone, offering a non-invasive diagnostic approach (Fig. 3II). In response to infection, US stimulation triggers drug release by inducing the rupture of poly (L-lactic acid) (PLLA) microcapsules embedded in the gel. These microcapsules release cefazolin sodium, an antibiotic, upon exposure to US. Under US intensity of 200 W/cm2, approximately 90% of the drug was released within 16 min, compared with less than 5% release in the absence of US stimulation. This approach significantly enhanced bacterial eradication in infected wounds.

Controlled biomaterial degradation is critical to accommodate variations in the tissue regeneration capacity of patients, which depends on individual factors such as age, disease state, nutritional status, lifestyle, and gender. To address this, researchers (DeBari et al., 2021) proposed a novel method using therapeutic US to non-invasively regulate the degradation behavior of filamentous fibronectin biomaterials after implantation. The oscillation of microbubbles generates mechanical forces through acoustic cavitation that precisely weaken the scaffold architecture, thereby accelerating degradation without impairing cellular viability. This dynamic degradation capability enables scaffolds to adapt to the varying regenerative demands of individual patients. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) revealed significant surface structural changes following ultrasonic treatment (Fig. 3III A, Fig. 3III B). Quantitative analysis demonstrated that 15-min US exposure reduced pore wall thickness compared with non-sonicated controls (Fig. 3III C, Fig. 3III D). Ahmad et al. (2021) developed an on-demand drug delivery scaffold in which ultrasonic cavitation-induced microbubble rupture exerts localized mechanical stress on embedded microcapsules, thereby enabling controlled drug release. This system incorporates gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and iron nanoparticles (FeNPs) into separate microcapsules embedded with a collagen-glycosaminoglycan (CG) scaffold (Fig. 3IV). Sequential release of therapeutic agents is achieved as US selectively triggers each type of nanoparticle, thereby mimicking the temporal pattern of growth factor or signaling molecule release during the natural wound healing process.

US-responsive scaffolds offer a spatiotemporally controllable therapeutic tool for wound healing through precise drug release and adaptive degradation modulation. These systems integrate multidimensional capabilities, including on-demand drug release, real-time wound monitoring, synergistic antimicrobial activity, and controllable degradation, collectively enhancing treatment precision and efficacy. However, several challenges persist. In terms of mechanical stability and durability, hydrogels often exhibit insufficient mechanical properties, which may hinder fibroblast adhesion and proliferation(Gounden and Singh, 2024; Wang et al., 2024b). Regarding biocompatibility and safety, residual cross-linking agents or toxic degradation by-products from scaffold materials may induce local cytotoxicity and inflammatory responses, ultimately delaying wound healing (Olteanu et al., 2024).

Moreover, from a clinical practicality standpoint, the portability and cost of ultrasound devices are critical factors in outpatient and home care settings. Therapeutic ultrasound probes for wound management are typically handheld, with transducer heads ranging from 2 to 5 cm in diameter. Several battery-powered, portable systems are commercially available (e.g., SonicHeal®, MIST Therapy®), allowing for patient self-administration or use by community healthcare professionals (Wang et al., 2024a). The cost of these devices varies significantly: portable low-intensity units range from $2000 to $5000 USD, whereas console-based high-intensity systems may exceed $15,000 USD. Disposable coupling patches or applicators entail additional per-treatment expenses (Chang et al., 2025). Comprehensive cost-effectiveness evaluations relative to standard care, as well as assessments of long-term healthcare savings, are required to support broader clinical adoption.

sec.title: Ultrasound-activated piezoelectric materials
sec.p:
The interaction between US and piezoelectric materials offers inherent advantages for wound healing applications. When US propagates through a medium, it generates mechanical vibrations that can remotely activate piezoelectric materials. Piezoelectric materials convert mechanical energy into localized electrical stimulation via the piezoelectric effect. Piezoelectric technology enhances wound healing by generating endogenous-like electrical fields under ultrasound stimulation, which promote cellular processes such as fibroblast migration and proliferation through the upregulation of VEGF, FGF-2, PI3K, and ERK (Fig. 4I A). Additionally, piezoelectric materials produce antibacterial reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as •OH and •O₂− (Fig. 4I B), effectively targeting infection and supporting a sterile healing microenvironment.Fig. 4
Schematic illustration of ultrasound-induced piezoelectricity and electrical stimulation for promoting wound healing. I. Mechanisms of electrical stimulation in wound repair: A) Cellular and tissue-level effects. B) Antimicrobial mechanism. Reprinted with permission from Wei et al. (2025). II. Applications of piezoelectric materials in bacteria elimination (A), immunomodulation (B), and wound healing (C). Reprinted with permission from Wang et al. (2024a). III. A) Digital photos showing the output voltage generated from the P(VDF–TrFE)/1.5BT membrane on the mouse skin. B) Schematic illustration of the P(VDF–TrFE)/BT membrane under ultrasonic stimulation. C) Nanofibrous microstructure of the P(VDF–TrFE)/1.5BT membranes. D) Output voltage generated from the ultrasound-activable P(VDF–TrFE)/1.5BT membrane on mouse skin. Reprinted with permission from Shi et al. (2022).Fig. 4

Piezoelectric materials developed via electrospinning exhibit excellent flexibility and favorable piezoelectric properties. Wang et al. (2025b) fabricated composite microfibers by modifying modified Li-doped ZnO/PLLA with 4 - octyl itaconate (4OI). During the infection stage, the Li-doped ZnO nanorods were activated by US-triggered acoustic kinetic therapy to generate ROS, which synergized with the release of metal cations and the positive electrical output from the piezoelectric fibers to exert antimicrobial effects (Fig. 4II A). The system elicited significant antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects through the sustained release of 4OI and bioactive metal ions, which work by scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) and modulating key inflammatory pathways (Fig. 4II B). During the proliferation and remodeling phases, the incorporated mild electrical stimulation synergistically promoted angiogenesis and neuronal regeneration (Fig. 4II C). This “time-dependent therapy” strategy overcomes the static functional limitations of traditional wound dressings by enabling stage-specific intervention throughout the healing process. Shi et al. (2022) developed flexible piezoelectric membranes composed of poly(vinylidene fluoride-trifluoroethylene) copolymer (P(VDF-TrFE)) and barium titanate (BaTiO3, BT) using the electrospinning technique (Fig. 4III B, Fig. 4III C). These composite membranes are capable of converting ultrasonic energy into electrical energy, demonstrating effective energy transduction performance. Experimental results showed that the ultrasonic intensity (ranging from 0.5 to 2.5 W/cm2) was positively correlated with the output current and voltage, indicating tunable piezoelectric response under different stimulation conditions (Fig. 4III D). Electrical stimulation generated significantly enhanced fibroblast proliferation and migration, leading to accelerated wound closure in the animal models. The combination of US and piezoelectric fibrous materials exhibited significant therapeutic effects at different stages of the wound healing process.

In addition, piezoelectric materials can be integrated with various complementary technologies, such as acoustic dynamics, photodynamic therapy (PDT), and pH responsiveness, to achieve multimodal smart dressing performance. Zhu et al. (2023) designed a composite system comprising barium titanate (BTO), zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8), and ciprofloxacin (CIP) to act synergistically as antimicrobial agents and tissue repair promoters. Specifically, under ultrasonic stimulation, the piezoelectric BTO generates electrical signals that, along with Zn2+ ions released from the degradation of ZIF-8, promote cell proliferation and tissue regeneration. BTO, a piezoelectric material, produces an electric field in response to US-induced mechanical stress. Additionally, it reacts with the surrounding water molecules and oxygen, generating hydroxyl radicals (-OH) and superoxide anion radicals (-O₂-) and forming highly oxidative reactive ROS. ZIF-8 is degraded in the acidic environment of infected wounds, facilitating the targeted release of CIP and enhancing localized antibacterial efficacy. Chen et al. (2025a) developed NiNb₂O₆ (NNO) nanomaterials, a class of semiconductors capable of generating ROS with potent oxidative properties upon exposure to simulated solar radiation (SSR), rendering them suitable for PDT. Furthermore, these materials exhibit piezoelectric properties, making them amenable to combined US and light-driven therapeutic strategies. Through the synergistic use of SSR to activate the PDT effect and ultrasonic stimulation to induce the piezoelectric effect, a dual photodynamic-piezocatalytic therapeutic effect is achieved, significantly accelerating wound healing. The design of piezoelectric hydrogels enhances the biocompatibility of piezoelectric materials and supports their activation by multiple stimuli (e.g., US, heat, and magnetic fields). However, the fabrication of piezoelectric materials requires complex processes and technologies, such as electrospinning and 3D printing, which increase the production cost and time requirements, thereby limiting their scalability and broader clinical application. In addition, the biological response to piezoelectric material stimulation may vary among individuals, making it challenging to predict and control therapeutic efficacy across diverse patient populations. This variation presents a significant barrier to the standardization and consistency of treatment. Most experimental studies are currently confined to animal models, and the potential differences in human tissue responses remain unclear, necessitating further investigation and clinical translational research (Alvarez-Lorenzo et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2025).

sec.title: Ultrasound-responsive microbubbles
sec.p:
US-responsive microbubbles represent a novel class of drug delivery systems, primarily functioning through the mechanism of acoustic cavitation, including stable and inertial cavitation (Fig. 5I). Microbubbles undergo rapid expansion and collapse during inertial cavitation, producing microjets and shock waves that perforate adjacent cell membranes and disrupt surrounding vasculature, thereby increasing vascular permeability (Fig. 5II). Under US irradiation, microbubbles generate mechanical forces through acoustic cavitation, enabling the controlled release of their encapsulated contents, including therapeutic gases (Fig. 5II) (Al-Jawadi and Thakur, 2020). In diabetic patients, microangiopathy leads to reduced blood flow at wound sites, resulting in inadequate oxygen supply. This hypoxic environment delays cell migration, neovascularisation, and collagen deposition, while promoting bacterial infection and sustaining chronic inflammation, creating a vicious cycle. Therefore, improving local oxygen supply becomes a key to treatment.Fig. 5
Response principle and application model diagram of microbubbles I. Stable and inertial cavitation. Reprinted with permission from Al-Jawadi and Thakur (2020). II. Effect of ultrasound-induced cavitation on cell membrane permeability. Reprinted with permission from Al-Jawadi and Thakur (2020). III. A) Schematic illustration of HP/OMBs + US for wound healing in diabetic foot ulcers. Reprinted with permission from Zhuge et al. (2025). B Schematic illustration of MnCDs@O2MBs preparation with dual oxygen supply capability for enhanced SDT in diabetic wound healing. Reprinted with permission from Ma et al. (2025).Fig. 5

Zhuge et al. (2025) developed heparin-poloxam temperature-sensitive hydrogels (HP/OMBs) encapsulating oxygen microbubbles. The system remains in a sol state at room temperature and undergoes gelation at body temperature, facilitating localized retention of microbubbles at the wound site. Upon US stimulation, the microbubbles rupture and release oxygen directly into the deep wound layers, enhancing tissue oxygenation and promoting wound healing (Fig. 5III A). Compared with conventional hyperbaric oxygen therapy, HP/OMBs systems mitigate the risk of systemic oxygen toxicity and promote endothelial cell migration and ordered collagen deposition by ameliorating hypoxia. Ma et al. (2025) developed a chemico-physical dual oxygen delivery system comprising oxygen microbubbles (MnCDs@O₂MBs) loaded with manganese-doped carbon dots (MnCDs). The MnCDs function as both acoustic sensitizers and hydrogen peroxidase-mimicking catalysts, promoting the in situ generation of oxygen from H2O2 at the infection site. Oxygen stored in the microbubbles is simultaneously released via ultrasonic cavitation (Fig. 5III B). The addition of H₂O₂ significantly increases the concentration of ROS produced by SDT, and the combined cavitation effect disrupts the biofilm structure, contributing to improved sterilization outcomes.

Compared with hyperbaric oxygen therapy, oxygen microbubble systems avoid systemic oxygen toxicity while effectively sterilizing infected wounds. However, several limitations remain. First, wound healing is inherently prolonged, and the complex pathological microenvironment of chronic wounds may impact microbubble responsiveness, resulting in inconsistent therapeutic effects. Second, the in vivo stability and circulation duration of microbubbles are critical parameters that require further optimization to enhance treatment efficiency (Shakya et al., 2024). In addition, the resonant frequency of US-responsive microbubbles is influenced by multiple factors, including microbubble diameter, surrounding fluid density, ambient temperature, and external pressure (Shakya et al., 2024). Addressing these limitations requires the development of advanced fabrication systems capable of precisely controlling the microbubble diameter and composition, which is essential for achieving consistent and controllable therapeutic effects (Huang et al., 2023).

sec.title: Ultrasound-responsive microneedles
sec.p: Traditional microneedles have demonstrated clinical efficacy in scar treatment, cosmetic skincare, and chronic wound management due to their unique capacity for localized drug delivery, minimal invasiveness, and targeted therapeutic action (Hu et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2025; Yang et al., 2019). When combined with US, the therapeutic efficacy of microneedles is further enhanced. US significantly enhances the permeability of channels formed by microneedles through mechanical effects, thereby accelerating the diffusion and release of therapeutic agents and facilitating the delivery of drugs required for wound healing.

Microneedle-induced physical puncture disrupts biofilm structures and enables the delivery of acoustic sensitizers. Upon activation by US, ROS are generated, which synergistically enhances the eradication and clearance of deep-seated biofilms (Fig. 6I). Ouyang et al. (2023) developed an advanced microneedle system using TiO₂ crystalline phase engineering, encapsulating TiO₂ nanoparticles within hyaluronic acid (HA) microneedles. Under US excitation (1 MHz, 1.5 W/cm2, 15 min, 50% duty cycle), these microneedles rapidly dissolved and released nanoparticles while simultaneously activating TiO₂’s photocatalytic properties. US excitation caused the electrons of TiO₂ on the surface of AB-TiO₂ to jump from the valence band to the conduction band, thus promoting ROS generation. The microneedle system penetrated biofilms, enabling non-invasive treatment of deep-seated infections (Fig. 6 III B). Cao et al. (2025) developed a composite microneedle loaded with glucose oxidase (GOX) and the oxygen vacancies TiO₂-X (MN@GOX@ TiO₂-X@CO) (Fig. 6II A, Fig. 6II B). This system released CO gas via a cascade reaction triggered by US (1.75 W/cm2, 10 min) (Fig. 6II C), achieving over 95% killing efficiency against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA). Under US irradiation, MN@GOX@TiO2-X@CO demonstrated effective antibacterial activity against Staphylococcus aureus, as evidenced by reduced colony counts and prominent red fluorescence from propidium iodide-stained dead bacteria (Fig. 6III A). Xiang et al. (2023) constructed US-responsive microneedles via interfacial engineering by compositing zinc porphyrin metal-organic frameworks (ZnTCPP) and ZnO. After 15 min of US irradiation (1.0 MHz, 1.5 W/cm2), the ZnTCPP@ZnO nanoparticles were able to adsorb oxygen molecules, which transitioned more efficiently from the ground state to the excited state due to interfacial effects. This process resulted in the generation of abundant ROS, achieving an antimicrobial efficiency of 99.73% against propionibacterium acnes (Xiang et al., 2023).Fig. 6
Mode of action and characterization of ultrasound-responsive microneedles. I. Model diagram of the synergistic anti-biofilm effect of ultrasound and microneedles. Reprinted with permission from Ouyang et al. (2023). II. A) Diagram of the mode of action of MN@GOX@TiO2-X@CO. B) SEM image of MN@GOX@TiO2-X@CO. C) Real-time monitoring of CO release from MN@GOX@TiO2-X@CO using UV spectroscopy (path length: 10 mm). Reprinted with permission from Cao et al. (2025). III. A) Staphylococcus aureus was subjected to colony counting and propidium iodide fluorescence staining using MN@GOX@TiO₂-X@CO with/without ultrasonic treatment. B, C) Illustration of the possible antibacterial mechanism of CuO2/TiO2. Reprinted with permission from Ouyang et al. (2023), Cao et al. (2025) and Liang et al. (2023).Fig. 6

To further enhance antimicrobial kinetic efficiency, Liang et al. (2023) developed a CuO₂/TiO₂ heterostructured microneedle system that integrates sonochemical dynamic therapy (SCDT) with acoustic thermotherapy. This was achieved through the synergistic interaction between copper perovskite nanoclusters and oxygen-rich, vacancy-engineered titanium dioxide. The design not only narrowed the band gap of TiO₂ to enhance the acoustic sensitization effect but also facilitated the efficient conversion of H₂O₂ into ROS via the Fenton catalysis of CuO₂. This resulted in a clearance rate exceeding 99% of multidrug-resistant pathogens within 5 min (Fig. 6III C), representing the most rapid in vivo antimicrobial efficacy reported to date. Notably, all of the above studies leverage the mechanical penetration capability of microneedles to overcome biological barriers while optimizing the acoustic responsiveness through material engineering. This dual-track strategy significantly enhances the precision and effectiveness of therapeutic interventions.

Notably, the literature consistently uses hyaluronic acid (HA) is a common material for synthesizing microneedles and encapsulating therapeutic agents. HA-based microneedles offer several advantages: they protect antimicrobial drugs from degradation by skin-surface enzymes, thereby prolonging drug stability and therapeutic duration, and they have consistently demonstrated favorable responsiveness to US stimulation across the literature. Microneedles are also capable of mechanically disrupting biofilms, enabling the localized delivery of high drug concentrations directly to sites of deep bacterial infection beyond the superficial biological barriers (Haidari et al., 2024). However, several challenges limit the clinical application of microneedle technology. For instance, microneedles are susceptible to fracture and retention within the skin due to high insertion resistance or improper handling, which can trigger inflammation, infection, and other adverse reactions. These limitations restrict their application in high-precision therapeutic scenarios. Additionally, microneedles often exhibit limited mechanical durability, making them prone to abrasion and deformation. This restricts their reuse potential and renders them more suitable for single-use applications, thereby increasing healthcare costs and contributing to resource waste. Clinical trials for microneedles in antimicrobial therapy are relatively scarce, and large-scale clinical data to support their safety and efficacy remain limited (Bhatnagar et al., 2017).

sec.title: Ultrasound-responsive flexible patch
sec.p: The advancement of flexible electronics has enabled the development of a new generation of smart wound therapy patches. These devices offer superior conformability and comfort, enabling close integration with the skin and facilitating personalized wound treatment. In addition, the tunability of multiple physical fields (e.g., electrical, thermal, and mechanical stimuli) significantly enhances the application potential of flexible electronics in wound care, allowing for more targeted and adaptable therapeutic strategies.

Ultrasonic patches require a relatively complex integrated design to optimize energy conversion efficiency. Xue et al. (2024) developed a flexible ultrasonic electrically coupled patch whose response mechanism is based on the coupling of the piezoelectric effect and ultrasonic mechanical energy. When an external transmitter emits US waves (1 MHz, 0.1 W/cm2), the sound waves are transmitted through an aqueous medium to the patch, inducing periodic vibrations of the γ-glycine/PVA film (Fig. 7I A). These mechanical vibrations are converted into charge separation via the piezoelectric effect (Fig. 7II A). The charge accumulates between the platinum electrodes on the film's upper and lower surfaces, generating an electric field of approximately 220 mV/mm. Animal studies demonstrated that this device reduced the wound healing time by 40% (Fig. 7III A). Chen et al. (2025b) designed and fabricated a flexible US-electrically coupled patch (UEP) incorporating a rigid transducer element. This system integrates US focusing for deep tissue stimulation and acoustic-to-electric energy conversion functions (Fig. 7I B). The US array system utilizes high-frequency alternating current (420 kHz, 10–20 Vpp) to excite the inverse piezoelectric effect in PZT-4, generating therapeutic US waves capable of penetrating up to 4 cm into wound tissue (Fig. 7II B). The US intensity, which is regulated by the input voltage, directly promotes deep tissue healing (Fig. 7III B), while the unabsorbed US energy is converted into pulsed voltage through an energy exchange array via the direct piezoelectric effect of PZT-4. Beyond therapeutic delivery, the system incorporates an innovative monitoring capability through impedance measurement. The coil structure integrated into the device enables real-time tracking of wound recovery by capturing and analyzing changes in electrical skin resistance at the wound site. Physiological changes during the healing process, such as changes in tissue moisture, collagen density, and inflammatory state, significantly affect local electrical resistance. These changes can be visualized using an external galvanometer. Abnormal resistance fluctuations may indicate infection or delayed healing, thereby enabling early clinical intervention.Fig. 7
Ultrasonic Patch: Structure and Wound Healing Promotion I. Design schematic diagrams of the two types of ultrasonic patches. Reprinted with permission from Xue et al. (2024) and Chen et al. (2025a). II. Schematic diagrams of the electrical response mechanisms of the two types of ultrasonic patches. Reprinted with permission from Xue et al. (2024) and Chen et al. (2025b). III. In vivo wound healing rates in animals treated with the two types of ultrasound patches. Reprinted with permission from Xue et al. (2024) and Chen et al. (2025a).Fig. 7

The deformability of US patches not only enables close conformity to wound contours but also allows the focusing of US waves for more precise therapeutic outcomes. Lyu et al. (2021) developed a flexible US patch (20 mW/cm2 for 20 min) for chronic wound repair. An external signal generator applied an alternating voltage (<10 V) to piezoelectric ceramic units, which vibrated to generate US waves at a frequency of approximately 2.2 MHz. By adjusting the bending angle of the piezoelectric cells arranged into a curved structure, the system achieved a focused US energy directed toward the deep tissues. This design effectively minimized energy scattering, which is a common issue associated with traditional rigid US probes that fail to conform to irregular skin surfaces.

Currently, US patches used for wound treatment function primarily through acoustic-electric coupling based on the piezoelectric effect. Among the materials used in the synthesis of US patches, piezoelectric ceramics stand out due to their high piezoelectric coefficients (d₃₃), typically in the range of 300–700 pC/N, and their high electromechanical coupling coefficients, which facilitate efficient mutual conversion between acoustic and electrical energy (Alvarez-Lorenzo et al., 2023). Although the size of the patches can be adjusted to some extent, their capacity to cover large or irregularly shaped wounds remains limited. In addition, the efficacy of acoustic-electrical conversion may be diminished by the complex in vivo environment. Additionally, long-term use could lead to material aging, electrode fatigue, and changes in the performance of the hydrogel coupling layer, factors that require further investigation.

sec.title: Multifunctional design paradigms and inherent trade-offs
sec.p: The five classes of ultrasound-responsive materials summarized in this review, although differing in structure and activation mechanisms, share several fundamental design challenges stemming from the multifunctional requirements of modern wound care. As previously noted, an effective wound dressing must simultaneously fulfill material performance criteria and promote wound healing functions (Fig.1). Refinement of these designs is essential for advancing from proof-of-concept prototypes to clinically viable therapeutic applications (Chen et al., 2024; Yuan et al., 2023).

Exudate Management versus Acoustic Fidelity: Chronic wounds frequently generate substantial exudate, requiring dressings with high fluid absorption capacity. Hydrogel-based scaffolds and certain flexible patches have demonstrated particular efficacy in managing such conditions (Feng et al., 2024; Mehmood et al., 2025). High water content and swelling, which enable effective fluid handling, can significantly attenuate and scatter ultrasound waves, reducing energy delivery to deeper tissues and impairing the precision of drug release (Perolina et al., 2024). To address this challenge, material engineers are investigating advanced design strategies, including asymmetric or gradient porosity architectures that direct fluid away from the acoustic interface, integration of acoustic impedance-matching layers (e.g., silicone membranes), and development of composite systems in which a thin, acoustically transparent therapeutic layer is functionally integrated with a distinct absorbent substrate.

Therapeutic Payload versus Structural Integrity: A core promise of these systems is the localized delivery of high-concentration therapeutics (antibiotics, growth factors, oxygen). Maximizing drug loading, however, can plasticize polymer matrices (e.g., in microneedles or scaffold walls) or destabilize encapsulation systems (e.g., microbubble shells), compromising their mechanical strength. This vulnerability is exacerbated by the mechanical stresses of US-induced cavitation and streaming, which can cause premature structural failure(Chen et al., 2023). Advanced material designs address this through nanoparticle encapsulation prior to incorporation, dynamic covalent cross-linking in hydrogels that can self-heal, and hierarchical structuring to isolate the drug reservoir from load-bearing components.

Biomechanical Conformability versus Functional Reliability: Effective wound contact is paramount for energy transfer, biosensing, and patient comfort, especially for flexible patches designed for prolonged wear. This requires materials that are soft, stretchable, and adaptable. However, such mechanical compliance can degrade the performance of functional elements: repeated bending may crack brittle piezoelectric ceramics (e.g., PZT), delaminate thin-film electrodes, or alter the resonant frequency of embedded acoustic components (Wang et al., 2025a). The field of flexible bioelectronics is addressing these challenges via textile-integrated sensors, use of inherently flexible piezoelectric polymers (e.g., PVDF or its copolymers), and geometric engineering such as serpentine circuit layouts that accommodate strain without loss of function.

These interconnected design imperatives—managing the wound environment, delivering potent therapy, and maintaining robust function in a dynamic setting—form the central engineering dilemma for US-responsive wound platforms (Chen et al., 2024). The material innovations summarized in Table 2 each represent a unique approach to balancing these priorities. Ultimately, the clinical translation and widespread adoption of these technologies will depend not only on their therapeutic efficacy but also on how elegantly they resolve these practical design conflicts. Therefore, a deep understanding and innovative resolution of these core trade-offs are pivotal for advancing these dressings from the laboratory to the clinic and the marketplace.

sec.title: Conclusion and outlook
sec.p: This systematic review provides a comprehensive analysis of the transformative potential of US and US-responsive materials in advancing wound healing therapeutics. Ultrasound directly promotes wound healing by stimulating cellular activities to accelerate tissue formation and repair (LIPUS), while simultaneously clearing necrotic tissue and disrupting biofilms through mechanical and thermal effects (HIFU), optimizing the wound environment for regeneration. US-responsive materials harness unique US-induced physical mechanisms, such as cavitation, acoustic streaming, radiation forces, and piezoelectric effects, to enable precise spatiotemporal drug delivery, improve biofilm penetration, modulate immune responses, and enhance oxygenation in hypoxic tissues. Collectively, these mechanisms contribute to significant improvements in the wound healing process.

The therapeutic potential of 5 US-responsive materials in wound healing is highlighted in this review, with each material demonstrating distinct therapeutic advantages. Spatiotemporally controlled drug release is enabled by scaffolds through US-triggered cavitation and mechanical vibrations. These scaffolds are designed to undergo dynamic degradation, thereby adapting to individualized healing requirements. Piezoelectric materials convert US mechanical energy into bioelectric signals through the piezoelectric effect. This bioelectric stimulation modulates cell membrane potential, promoting cell migration and proliferation within a “temporal therapy” framework. Microbubbles undergo cavitation collapse under US, releasing oxygen and drugs to alleviate hypoxia and synergize with sonodynamic therapy. Microneedles mechanically penetrate biofilms, activating sonosensitizers under US to generate ROS for deep-tissue infection clearance and localized high-concentration drug delivery. Flexible US patches transduce acoustic energy into electrical signals via the piezoelectric effect, enabling real-time wound monitoring through resistance changes while adhering to tissue to focus US and accelerate healing. Despite these advantages, several limitations, including inadequate mechanical strength, complex fabrication procedures, poor in vivo stability, susceptibility to fracture and residual accumulation, and limited size adaptability, hinder broader clinical application. Among these, poor in vivo stability stands out as a pivotal translational barrier—one inherently linked to the therapeutic mechanism of ultrasound itself. Future research should prioritize the optimization of material composition, mechanical robustness, and biocompatibility to enhance therapeutic performance and support successful clinical translation.

The mechanical and thermal stresses induced by ultrasound during therapeutic application are the primary drivers of material instability, posing direct and significant challenges to the structural and functional integrity of ultrasound-responsive systems. Key destabilizing mechanisms include cavitation-induced shock waves and microjets, which can fracture brittle components and disrupt polymer networks, as well as acoustic streaming, which generates erosive shear forces (Zhai et al., 2022). Additionally, localized heating may alter the physical state of temperature-sensitive materials. Together, these factors compromise system reliability: hydrogels may undergo accelerated or non-uniform erosion, impairing controlled release; microbubble shells may leak prematurely or exhibit inconsistent collapse thresholds; piezoelectric elements can degrade due to micro-crack propagation; and microneedles risk untimely fracture. Tackling these stability challenges is crucial for the advancement of translational development. Strategies involving advanced materials, such as the use of mechanically reinforced nanocomposites, core-shell architectures to safeguard therapeutic cargo, and stimuli-responsive bonds that cleave selectively under specific ultrasound conditions, are essential for maintaining consistent performance throughout the material's therapeutic lifespan (Xiao et al., 2023). Furthermore, establishing standardized stability testing protocols under ultrasound exposure conditions that mirror clinical scenarios is imperative for achieving regulatory approval and facilitating the clinical application of these materials.

The clinical translation of ultrasound-responsive materials requires navigating a complex landscape of regulatory, manufacturing, and clinical validation challenges. A primary hurdle lies in regulatory classification, as these systems fall under two distinct regulatory paradigms. Those whose principal mode of action is physical—such as piezoelectric patches delivering electrical stimulation or scaffolds providing structural support—are typically classified as medical devices (Lu et al., 2025). In contrast, systems designed for the controlled release of therapeutic agents—such as drug-loaded microbubbles or antibiotic-releasing microneedles—are categorized as drug-delivery systems or combination products, subject to more rigorous pharmaceutical regulatory requirements. This classification directly shapes the scope of preclinical testing and clinical trial design. To demonstrate safety and efficacy, standardized evaluation protocols must be established, including comprehensive biocompatibility and biodistribution studies in accordance with standards such as ISO 10993, as well as precise calibration of acoustic parameters—including intensity, frequency, and spatial distribution—to ensure reproducible biological effects and material performance. Moreover, adherence to Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP) is essential to enable scalable, consistent production of these advanced materials, ensuring batch-to-batch reproducibility—a critical challenge for complex formulations such as piezoelectric nanofibers or engineered microbubbles. Finally, clinical trial design for combined ultrasound-material therapies poses unique methodological challenges (Zhang et al., 2025). These include appropriate patient stratification based on wound etiology, microbial profile, and perfusion status; the definition of clinically meaningful endpoints beyond simple wound closure rate, such as healing quality, recurrence rates, and patient-reported outcomes; and the development of robust blinding protocols to distinguish active from sham ultrasound exposure. Overcoming these translational barriers is imperative to advance these promising technologies from benchside innovation to clinical reality.

In conclusion, US-responsive materials represent a significant paradigm shift in wound care, transitioning from passive treatment approaches to dynamic, precision-guided therapeutic strategies. Despite ongoing translational challenges, their ability to remotely and noninvasively modulate biological processes positions them as critical enablers of accelerated healing, particularly in chronic or treatment-resistant wounds. As research advances and current limitations are addressed, US-responsive materials are poised to play an increasingly important role in the evolving landscape of wound healing therapeutics.
